title: Intersight Server Alarm Monitoring (Cisco Intersight cloud)
exclusions: Pure UCS Manager–only estates where the open-fault list you trust day-to-day is still classic UCSM `cisco:ucs:faults` in Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs, with no Intersight claim, belong in UC-19.1.1 instead of this cond/Alarms path. Air-gapped data halls or “no Intersight connector” policy positions where the REST alarm feed never leaves the facility, so Splunk cannot see `cisco:intersight:alarms`. UCS classic domains that were never claimed in Intersight, meaning there is no `RegisteredDevice` scope for the hardware in the cloud list. HyperFlex-first operational teams that intentionally route storage- and data-platform alarms only through HCI SRE dashboards, while still on Intersight, may need a child panel filter so this UC’s server-centric bridge is not the default. SaaS-only lab tenants or audit sandboxes with synthetic or demo content should not be merged into a production Splunk index used for sev-1 pages. Multi-tenant Intersight service providers: if a single org aggregates unrelated customers, separate Splunk indexes per tenant before you interpret cross-domain blast-radius columns, or the statistics lie about “your” estate. Edge-only CIMC servers not attached to the management domain you Intersight-claim: hardware exists, Intersight alarms do not, until the claim workflow completes.
dataSources: `index=cisco_intersight` (or your org-standard name bound to the modular inputs) and **primary** sourcetype `cisco:intersight:alarms` for the Intersight `cond/Alarms` model ingested by `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` (Splunkbase 7828). The same add-on’s inventory stream `cisco:intersight:inventory` is the usual place to map MoRef fields like `MoId` and `RegisteredDevice` to a human name when you add an optional `lookup` in dashboards. The TA also ingests `cisco:intersight:audit_logs` and `cisco:intersight:metrics` in many deployments—use audit for Intersight workflow correlation when an alarm matches a change window, and metrics when you are tracing threshold-driven behaviour. Alarms are collected from your Intersight tenant using HTTPS from the forwarder, typically via a dedicated API key, through an appliance, virtual connector, or direct SaaS endpoint your subscription documents. When architecture discussions mention “Cisco Intersight cloud,” that is the control plane; the `cisco:intersight:alarms` events are what Splunk indexed after a successful API poll, not a second hop through UCSM XML. If a sourcetype rename is proposed in `local/props.conf`, keep the CIM-optional field aliases in the same app; do not fork the string without a migration cut-over.
app: Install the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Intersight on disk as **`Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight`** ([Splunkbase 7828](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953))—this on-disk and Splunkbase identifier is the canonical name; marketing text “Cisco Intersight Add-on” is the same product. Current TA builds are supported on Splunk Enterprise 9.x and 10.x and on Splunk Cloud. Authentication uses a Cisco Intersight API key pair: KeyId and Secret, generated in the Intersight UI and stored in Splunk’s credential facility or the TA’s encrypted account object—never a cleartext secret in version-controlled `local/inputs.conf`. The modular inputs page exposes alarm polling; confirm `index`, poll interval, and the endpoint hostname that match your Intersight deployment model. Search Heads and indexers that search this index should have the add-on (or a dependency app) for field knowledge unless your team centralises all TA knowledge in a single shared app. The TA version you pin should track Cisco’s current Splunkbase release notes: major bumps sometimes rename optional fields, which is why the saved SPL in this use case is defensive with `coalesce()`.
spl:
// Stage 0 — Intersight cond/Alarms in Splunk as sourcetype cisco:intersight:alarms (Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight, Splunkbase 7828; REST /api/v1/cond/Alarms family).
// Stage 1 — Normalise Intersight field spellings: camelCase from API vs snake_case from some TA builds, plus older alias affected_object_type.
// Stage 2 — Keep actionable severities only, exclude Cleared rows (case-robust) per operator policy.
// Stage 3 — Bucket time to 15m for dashboard alignment; rank severity for stable sort (Critical first).
// Stage 4 — Blast radius: per RegisteredDevice+AffectedMoType alarm volume; cross-domain context per AffectedMoType.
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:alarms" earliest=-24h@h
| eval Severity_raw=coalesce(Severity, severity, sev, "")
| eval Description=coalesce(Description, description, message, msg, "")
| eval Code=coalesce(Code, code, alarm_code, "")
| eval AffectedMoType=coalesce(AffectedMoType, affected_mo_type, AffectedMotype, affected_object_type, "")
| eval MoId=coalesce(MoId, mo_id, moid, "")
| eval RegisteredDevice=coalesce(RegisteredDevice, registered_device, registeredDevice, "")
| eval OriginSys=coalesce(OriginSys, origin_sys, originSys, "")
| eval Acknowledge=coalesce(Acknowledge, acknowledge, ack, "")
| where isnotnull(Severity_raw) AND Severity_raw!=""
| where lower(Severity_raw)!="cleared" AND !match(Severity_raw, "(?i)^cleared$")
| where match(lower(Severity_raw), "^(critical|warning)$")
| bin _time span=15m
| eval sev_l=lower(Severity_raw)
| eval severity_rank=case(sev_l="critical",1,sev_l="warning",2,1=1,9)
| eval Severity=case(sev_l="critical","Critical",sev_l="warning","Warning",1=1,Severity_raw)
| eventstats count as blast_alarms_same_regdev_motype by RegisteredDevice, AffectedMoType
| eventstats dc(RegisteredDevice) as motype_regdev_spread, count as motype_window_total by AffectedMoType
| sort 0 +severity_rank, -_time, RegisteredDevice, MoId
| table _time, Severity, severity_rank, RegisteredDevice, AffectedMoType, MoId, Description, Code, OriginSys, Acknowledge, blast_alarms_same_regdev_motype, motype_regdev_spread, motype_window_total
description: Distinct from UC-19.1.1, which surfaces `faultInst` on `cisco:ucs:faults` for one UCSM domain, 19.1.19 reads Intersight `cond/Alarms` as `cisco:intersight:alarms` via `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight`. The SPL `coalesce()`s `Severity`, `Description`, `Code`, `MoId`, `AffectedMoType`, `RegisteredDevice`, `OriginSys`, and `Acknowledge`, drops `Cleared`, keeps **Critical** and **Warning** only, `bin _time` 15m, `severity_rank` for sort, `eventstats` for blast, then a triage `table`.
value: A single Intersight organisation can list alarms for many UCS domains, IMM, X-Series, and HyperFlex even when the physical kit spans regions, so the estate-wide question (what is wrong anywhere we claim) lives here, while UC-19.1.1 still answers the single-cluster question through `cisco:ucs:faults` and `dn` paths for each UCSM you poll. Splunk gives you a durable, time-stamped `Code`+`Description`+`MoId` trail next to app and facility logs, which supports incident review and control evidence. The saved `spl`’s `RegisteredDevice` and `motype_regdev_spread` fields help separate a noisy one-domain burst from a cross-domain `AffectedMoType` pattern. In **mixed** Intersight and classic UCSM sites, you keep both use cases: different management planes, not duplicate hardware stories.
implementation: Deploy `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` with a read-capable Intersight API key, bind alarms to the approved `index` (commonly `cisco_intersight`) at roughly five-minute intervals, and save the `spl` from this file as a report. Add an alert on new Critical events with throttling on `RegisteredDevice` and `Code`, and place panels beside UC-19.1.1 where both UCSM and Intersight are in use.
visualization: A heat or bar panel of top `RegisteredDevice` by `blast_alarms_same_regdev_motype`; a `table` of open Criticals (`Severity`+`Code`+`Description`+`OriginSys`); a bar chart of `AffectedMoType` with `motype_regdev_spread` as a line overlay; a 15m `timechart` of new alarm rate; and a `table` of unacknowledged rows using `Acknowledge` empties for operator follow-up, with a drill-out link to the Intersight Alarms view.

references.title: Cisco Intersight REST API — introduction / overview
Cisco Intersight — product documentation and user guides (index)

knownFalsePositives:
• Intersight platform releases occasionally reclassify a given `Code` between **Critical** and **Warning**; a historical Splunk month may show a severity migration that the current Intersight UI no longer uses as the live label, so your paging rule should always consider `Code` plus `AffectedMoType`, not a bare severity string, before you rewrite an RCA.
• Bulk firmware, driver, or service-profile automation launched from Intersight produces a **predictable** multi-minute alarm band that is expected while Cisco soak timers and verification tasks run; correlate the window with the Intersight **audit** stream before a sev-1.
• Cloud-connector reconnection or proxy healing after a brownout can **batch** timestamps so Splunk’s `_time` cluster looks spiky; compare `_time` to the connector’s own health panel or `cisco:intersight:audit_logs` for disconnect rows.
• HyperFlex- or `storage.HyperFlexCluster` style `AffectedMoType` labels are real infrastructure alarms that may be **routed** to a different queue than B-series racking; a compute-only SRE filter is policy, not a data defect.
• An acknowledged row whose `LastTransitionTime` (or a timestamp field the TA includes) **ticks** without a human-recognisable state change can re-float to the top of a “newest first” `table` while still being owned in Intersight; do not re-page without checking `Acknowledge` semantics in your build.
• `MoId` that falls out of the latest `cisco:intersight:inventory` snapshot after a decommission still has a true alarm in `cisco:intersight:alarms`; the “missing name” in a lookup join is an inventory-lag false negative in the human column, not a fake alarm.
• Trial or **demo** Intersight orgs that share a Splunk stack with production can add charts that have no real asset; never mix org keys into a production index without an explicit boundary.
• `_time` in Splunk should reflect Intersight’s alarm time; if a manual `props` change mis-parses JSON, a morning “spike” can be a **clock** artefact—compare the raw `CreationTime` field (if present) in one event to the parsed `_time`.
• Intersight’s product **UI** alarm retention can be **shorter** than Splunk’s index retention, so a quarter-old report in Splunk may include alarms a console filter no longer shows; document for auditors that the indexed export is the long-lived evidence store.
• Intersight can emit **Info**-class threshold chatter from metric policies in some deployments; if your key normalises that into a surprising token, the severity filter in this `spl` is a front-line guard—tighten `props` if needed rather than blinding the feed.
• A single alarm row **re-fetched** after a TA restart can double-count in a `stats` without a dedup, while a raw `table` of events shows both lines; the blast columns in this `spl` use 15m bucketing, but a monthly KPI may still need a **dedup** policy your governance approves.
• `RegisteredDevice` null after an un-claim in Intersight is an inventory transition signal; triage to CMDB and claim workflow, not to “Splunk is wrong.”
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Product scope vs UC-19.1.1 — **Use both** this use case and UC-19.1.1 in mixed UCSM-classic plus Intersight-claimed environments; they are not duplicates. UC-19.1.1 reads classic UCS Manager `faultInst` rows as `cisco:ucs:faults` from **one** UCSM cluster at a time (on-prem XML API or syslog). UC-19.1.19 reads Intersight cloud-aggregated cond/Alarms as `cisco:intersight:alarms` from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Intersight (`Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight`, Splunkbase 7828). The same physical blade can therefore appear in two planes: deep per-domain `dn` in 19.1.1, and cross-domain `RegisteredDevice` / MoRef-style addressing here. The unique value in 19.1.19 is a **single pan-estate** alarm timeline for multi-site triage, compliance, and “what the management plane saw when” forensics, which UCSM cannot merge across independent clusters.
• Intersight architecture in one paragraph — a supported appliance, virtual connector, or cloud connection registers your UCS, HyperFlex, and stand-alone compute to the Intersight control plane. Alarms in the Intersight cond model are then **visible through REST** to Splunk when you enable the TA’s alarm input. The Splunk tier never replaces Intersight; it indexes what your credential can read, on the poll schedule you set.
• TA identity — on-disk directory name: `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight`. Do not point production searches at a mis-typed add-on name from older slide decks. Confirm in **Apps → Manage Apps** the green **Enabled** state on the forwarder that runs the modular input.
• API key — in Intersight, **Settings → API Keys → Generate API Key** (wording can vary by subscription). You receive a KeyId and Secret. Create a Splunk **secret** or use the TA configuration workflow so the private key is never in clear text in `inputs.conf` checked into source control. Rotate keys on the same schedule as other non-human integrations.
• RBAC — a read-only operator profile that can list `cond/Alarms` is enough for this UC. Do **not** grant full Organisation Admin to the Splunk service principal unless a security exception is recorded.
• HTTPS proxy — if the forwarder must use a forward proxy to reach `intersight.com` (or the customer-specific hostname your tenant shows), set system or Splunk proxy options per your platform so TLS intercept does not break certificate validation.
• Polling — typical alarm poll interval in the field is **five minutes**; never assume sub-minute freshness unless your TA build and Intersight rate limits were explicitly designed for it.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection (modular input)**
(1) Install `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` on the same class of node your organisation already uses for HTTPS modular inputs, usually a Heavy Forwarder, and install a copy on Search Heads for field discovery.
(2) In Splunk Web, **Settings → Data inputs** (or the Cisco Intersight add-on’s configuration page, which surfaces the same logical keys), add an Intersight account using your KeyId/Secret, pick the Intersight endpoint that matches your tenant (Cisco’s documentation lists SaaS, connected appliance, and similar targets), and enable the **Alarms** input type so events arrive as `sourcetype="cisco:intersight:alarms"`. Bind the destination `index` to the approved name—commonly `cisco_intersight`.
(3) File-driven example — copy a stanza pattern from the TA’s `default/inputs.conf` (never invent keys) into `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/local/inputs.conf`, set `disabled=0` on the Intersight stanza you need, and restart `splunkd` on that host. A representative skeleton you must reconcile **verbatim** to your shipped file looks like the following; replace all placeholders and confirm names against the package you have:

```ini
# Example pattern only—copy real stanza names and parameters from the TA version you installed.
# [some_stanza_label://Intersight_Alarms_Corp]
# disabled = 0
# account = <account_stanza>
# index = cisco_intersight
# interval = 300
# (additional vendor keys as shipped)
```

(4) Discovery probe after two runs: `index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:alarms" earliest=-4h | head 20` — in **Fields** verify you see at least one of `Severity` or `severity`, `Description` or `description`, and `MoId`/`mo_id`. If every field is lower-case, the TA normalised; if mixed, the saved SPL’s `coalesce()` lists cover you.
(5) Defensive field handling — the TA generally preserves Cisco’s camelCase, but do not bet dashboards on it forever. The primary `spl` in this use case always wraps `Severity`, `Description`, `Code`, `AffectedMoType`, `MoId`, `RegisteredDevice`, `OriginSys`, and `Acknowledge` with `coalesce()` to alternate spellings.
(6) Inventory companion — to enrich MoRef in dashboards, the same add-on’s inventory stream (`cisco:intersight:inventory`) is the usual place to `lookup` a human label for a device or domain object, subject to the fields your build exposes. Keep that join on dashboards, not on the first-run alert, so you do not page “unknown” for a missing join row during an outage.

**Step 2 — Create the search, the alert, and optional enrichment**
(1) Paste the `spl` field from this JSON into **Search**, run over **Last 24 hours** first, then save as a report.
(2) Pipeline intent — the search drops cleared alarms, normalises case, bins time to 15m buckets for trending-style viewing, and adds **blast-radius** eventstats. `blast_alarms_same_regdev_and_motype` is the per-pair count for “how noisy is this domain for this class?” while `regdevs_with_this_motype` and `motype_window_events` help separate “one site is loud” from “one object type is loud everywhere.”
(3) Optional enrichment — create `lookups/cisco_intersight_devices.csv` in your org-owned app and wire `transforms.conf` so you can `lookup` a friendly name for a `MoId` or a `RegisteredDevice` pointer. Minimum columns:

```csv
device_moid,friendly_name,location,owner,site_class
devicereg/12345,AMER-UCS-PDX-01,Portland OR DC1,ops-compute@example.com,production
devicereg/12346,AMER-HX-PDX-02,Portland OR DC1,storage@example.com,production
devicereg/22301,EMEA-FI-DUB-03,Dublin DC2,ops-compute@example.com,production
devicereg/33001,APAC-IMM-SIN-04,Singapore edge,field-services@example.com,retail
devicereg/44000,LAB-INTERSIGHT-TEST,Splunk lab,splunk-admins@example.com,nonprod
devicereg/55001,DR-UCS-ASH-05,Ashburn DR,ops-dr@example.com,disaster_recovery
```

(4) A companion code-frequency panel for operations review, **not a substitute for the main pipeline**:

```spl
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:alarms" earliest=-24h@h
| eval Code=coalesce(Code, code, alarm_code, "")
| eval Severity=coalesce(Severity, severity, "")
| eval Desc=coalesce(Description, description, "")
| where isnotnull(Code) AND match(lower(Severity), "^(critical|warning)$")
| stats count, values(Desc) as sample_desc by Code
| sort -count
| head 25
```

(5) Alert design — for paging, add `| search Acknowledge` empty tests only after you see how the TA populates `Acknowledge` in your build (empty string, null, or `false` string). Throttle on `MoId, Code` or `RegisteredDevice, AffectedMoType` to avoid one noisy sensor spamming the bridge.

**Step 3 — Validate against Intersight**
(1) Log in to the same Intersight organisation the Splunk credential uses, open the **Alarms** view, and set the time filter to the same 24h window (UTC vs local operator time — watch the banner).
(2) Pick a **Critical** row in Splunk, note `Code`, `MoId`, and a substring of `Description`, then find the same alarm in the Intersight UI. If the UI is quiet but Splunk is loud, you may be on a read-only sub-account with a smaller scope than the key Splunk uses—fix RBAC, not the SPL. If Splunk is quiet and the UI is loud, check whether the forwarder is rate-limited (Intersight documents default API rate windows; 1000 requests per hour is a common starting guardrail—your automation must not burst polling outside policy).
(3) If counts differ slightly, consider duplicate pulls after connector restart; resolve with a **dedup** on (`MoId`,`Code`, `_time` bucket) in reporting searches only after you read TA release notes, because an aggressive dedup can hide genuine re-arms.

**Step 4 — Operationalise**
(1) Dashboard layout — on mixed estates, create one dashboard row for **UCSM `cisco:ucs:faults`** (UC-19.1.1) and the **next** row for `cisco:intersight:alarms` (this UC) so the bridge sees both planes in one bookmark.
(2) RACI — platform compute owns the Intersight connector health; the Splunk team owns the TA version and **indexer** availability; application owners own workload impact statements when an alarm is vague.
(3) Alerting cadence — run every 5–15 minutes with a rolling 24h window, throttle per domain, and add a “maintenance in progress” label by joining an internal change calendar keyed on Intersight profile or workflow name when you adopt automated firmware campaigns.

**Step 5 — Tuning and troubleshooting**
• API key rotation without downtime — add the new key, switch the TA to it, then retire the old key after 24h of clean polls.
• HTTP 401/403 — the secret rotated or the Intersight account is locked; check Intersight audit first, then the TA log on the forwarder.
• 429 throttling from Intersight — lower auxiliary inputs or widen intervals; never stack five lab tenants on one key if they share a rate window.
• HyperFlex storage alarms in the same sourcetype — add `where NOT match(AffectedMoType, "(?i)HyperFlex")` on compute-only SLO boards if the noise is policy-approved.
• Field-case drift after TA upgrade — re-run a `fieldsummary` in a 1h window and extend `coalesce()` if Cisco adds a new alias.
• `Cleared` replay — if a bulk refresh re-sends a cleared transition, the `where` clause on cleared strings should still drop the row; if not, your tenant emits a different clear token—add it to the guard.
• Inventory lookup misses — the alarm is true even when CMDB is stale; do not code “drop if no lookup” on paging paths.
• Single-domain spike vs multi-domain code spike — use the pair-count columns from this UC’s `spl` before you assume a code defect; cross-domain code spikes are rare and worth a vendor case.
• Clock skew on search heads — NTP is mandatory when correlating to application logs; `_time` is the alarm time from Intersight, not the forwarder’s receipt in edge cases of backlog.
• Storage growth — the alarms index is usually modest versus wire data; if not, a mis-set **debug** input or an accidental `All MOs` debug flag in a lab TA can explode volume—treat a sudden 10× as a config incident.
• **Governance** — document which Intersight org the TA credential maps to. Pointing a production Splunk to a test org, or the inverse, is a classic audit finding.

(Additional operator notes) When Intersight adds new managed-object types to `AffectedMoType`, the visual distribution chart will grow new labels; that is a signal your estate changed, not that the SPL is wrong. Keep the companion **inventory** and **audit** documentation links in the same Confluence runbook for bridge engineers who need the workflow and not only the raw alarm.

**Step 2 (panel clone) — optional rate-of-change monitor**

```spl
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:alarms" earliest=-24h@h
| eval Severity=coalesce(Severity, severity, "")
| where match(lower(Severity), "^(critical|warning)$")
| timechart span=15m count by Severity
```

This clone does not replace the main `table`, but is useful in executive summaries.

When documentation references “affected object type” in old internal PDFs, treat that as plain-language for `AffectedMoType`; the canonical Intersight field name in the REST model is the same family as `AffectedMoType` or `affected_mo_type` in Splunk, which is why the `coalesce()` in this UC is intentionally wide. If a future minor TA release maps only snake_case, your defensive SPL still runs without a Friday-night props edit, though you should still add the vendor’s new field to `coalesce()` after you read the release note.

**Connector health cross-check** — the TA cannot alarm if the connector is offline. Mirror `cisco:intersight:audit_logs` in a small single-value “last successful poll time” if your team already normalises that stream; this UC does not require it, but mixed deployments with UC-19.1.1 benefit when both panels sit together.

**Evidence pack** — for auditors, export a scheduled PDF of the 24h `table` search monthly; the row mix of `RegisteredDevice` and `AffectedMoType` is the traceable “management-plane awareness” list without re-clicking through each Intersight org site by site. Pair that PDF with a change ticket index if you need to show mitigations in the same 24h window.

**Alert fatigue guard** — on day one, do not page Warning unless operations agrees; set two saved searches, one Critical-only and one Critical+Warning for daytime chat.

**Name collisions** — `MoId` is not globally unique across every Cisco cloud forever; scoping in search by `index`+`RegisteredDevice`+`MoId` is the safe triple. If your organisation reuses lab MoIds, keep lab on a different index or sourcetype filter.

**Final hand-off to ServiceNow or similar** — pass `Code`, all three blast columns, a deep link to Intersight Alarms (bookmark per org), and the CMDB `site_class` from the lookup. That ticket template prevents round-trips asking “which domain?”
If you are uncertain whether a row is a **server** alarm or a **fabric policy** object, the `AffectedMoType` string is the first place to look; compare against Cisco’s reference table for the cond model, not against UCSM’s `dn` path mental model, because the management planes differ by design. This is another angle on why UC-19.1.1 remains valuable for engineers who think in `sys/…/blade` paths, while **UC-19.1.19** is better for “which Intersight org, which `RegisteredDevice`, and which object type” triage.


Appendix — operational depth (keeps this use case implementation chapter self-contained for long-form audits)

A. Certificate and name-resolution hygiene — the forwarder must resolve the same hostname the TA is configured to call; split-horizon DNS with a “datacenter” view that differs from a laptop’s split VPN view has caused more false “Splunk is empty” pages than the SPL itself. Keep a one-line `nslookup` and `curl -Iv` in the runbook, stored next to the API key change procedure.

B. btool and precedence — as with any add-on, `local/inputs.conf` should override `default/`; never edit `default` in place. The operator command `splunk btool inputs list Intersight --debug` (exact stanza name varies) lets you print the **effective** key set after merge. If a supposed five-minute `interval` still behaves like thirty minutes, you are not editing the stanza the modular input is actually using.

C. Intersight throttling in practice — a rate window that applies to the API key is shared with other automation. If a campus lab script stress-tests the same org key every hour, the Splunk poller can starve. Split keys by **environment** in Intersight and in Splunk, not only by “who asked first.”

D. Deduplication policy — a duplicate row that differs only in `_indextime` is still one alarm from an operator’s perspective. Some teams add `| dedup MoId, Code, RegisteredDevice, AffectedMoType sortby - _time` in **reporting** only; the raw index stays intact for forensics. Document which saved search uses the dedup so auditors know you did not “hide” a second incident.

E. Sourcetype guard-rail — the canonical alarm sourcetype is `cisco:intersight:alarms`. A mistyped `cisco:intersight:alarm` (singular) in a copied dashboard is an empty search. A blue/green re-index migration might temporarily dual-write; union only during transition.

F. Indexer affinity — events are small, but a mis-set `TRUNC` or `max_events` in `props` from an unrelated app can break JSON fragments on the Heavy Forwarder; if you see half-field bodies, the fix is in `props` merge order, not the SPL. Use `| head 1` in verbose mode to inspect.

G. CIM / ES note — this UC is observability on management-plane cond alarms; the TA does not automatically mean Enterprise Security CIM is satisfied for “Hardware” the way some catalogues think about OS agents. If your programme maps a hardware story to a CIM `hardware` or custom datamodel, that is a separate line item, not a silent assumption.

H. Pager routing — a cross-domain `AffectedMoType` that touches `network.Element` is sometimes still a fabric policy story; route a copy of the alert to the networking bridge queue, not only compute, if your RACI table says so. The Intersight object type in the string is a better router than a keyword guess in `Description`.

I. What “blast radius” does not show — a low `motype_regdev_spread` with a very high `motype_window_total` on the same `AffectedMoType` can still be one broken automation sending many child rows; the statistics point you to ask whether the Intersight workflow engine is the common cause before you re-seat hardware in three cities.

J. Decommissioning — when a domain unclaims, `RegisteredDevice` can go null on new rows while historical rows still sort to last week. Filter “null domain but non-null `MoId`” into an inventory work queue, not a false positive queue.
grandmaExplanation: Intersight keeps one shared alarm list in the cloud for every data centre and domain you connect, so you can see one noisy site or a wide pattern in the same place before a fault spreads.
hardware: Cisco UCS B200 M6/M7, C220, C240, C480 rack models, X210c, X410c, UCS 6300/6400/6500 family Fabric Interconnects (e.g. 6454, 6536), IOM 2200/2400, 9100 IFM, HyperFlex HX series where Intersight claims the cluster, and IMM-mode stand-alone servers once registered—classic UCSM-claimed, Intersight-led, and mixed profiles all surface through the same Intersight alarm object model when the device is in scope of your tenant’s claim.
